summary: Ancient Greek sculptor (c. 480–430 BC), best known for the chryselephantine Statue of Zeus at Olympia (one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World) and the chryselephantine Athena Parthenos inside the Parthenon. Supervised the creation of the Elgin Marbles. Name inspired Mark Barr's symbol phi for the golden ratio.

works.name: Statue of Zeus at Olympia
works.description: A massive chryselephantine (gold and ivory) seated statue of Zeus, one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World, commissioned by the Eleans for the Temple of Zeus at Olympia. Pausanias claims it wore a glass robe and held a Nike figurine. Napoleon's pose in Ingres's painting Napoleon I on His Imperial Throne was modeled on descriptions of this statue. Phidias was apocryphally murdered after completing it. The statue was repeatedly rubbed with olive oil because of the Altis Grove's marshiness. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Chryselephantine seated statue of Zeus at Olympia, enthroned holding a scepter; one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World
examples: name this Athenian sculptor of the Statue of Zeus at Olympia and of the Athena in the Parthenon.
This ancient Greek sculptor who depicted an enthroned deity holding a scepter in the Statue of Zeus at Olympia.
The citizens of nearby Elis commissioned a seated, chryselephantine sculpture by Phidias for this site's Temple of Zeus.

clues.clue: Pausanias claims the statue wore a glass robe and held a figurine of Nike
examples: Pausanias claims that this sculpture wore a glass robe and held a figurine of Nike.

clues.clue: Napoleon's pose in Ingres's Napoleon I on His Imperial Throne was modeled on descriptions of this statue
examples: In Ingres's painting, Napoleon's pose is modeled on descriptions of this sculpture.

clues.clue: Phidias was apocryphally murdered after completing it; it was rubbed with olive oil due to the Altis Grove's marshiness
examples: Phidias was apocryphally murdered after completing that chryselephantine statue in this city.
An artwork in this city was repeatedly rubbed with olive oil due to the Altis Grove's marshiness.

works.name: Athena Parthenos
works.description: Colossal chryselephantine statue of Athena housed in the naos of the Parthenon in Athens. About 41 feet tall. The artist and his patron Pericles appear on her circular shield. A coiled snake resided between the shield and her feet. She holds a Nike in her right hand. Her triple-crested helmet features a sphinx flanked by two pegasi. The Amazonomachy appears on her shield. Alan LeQuire recreated it in plaster for the naos of the Parthenon replica in Nashville. The Varvakeion marble is a surviving small-scale copy. Evidence of its original bright colors was erased when the British Museum cleaned the Elgin Marbles. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Chryselephantine (~41-foot) statue of Athena in the naos of the Parthenon; holds a Nike; right arm supported by a column
examples: This artist and his patron Pericles appear on the shield of a chryselephantine sculpture by him wearing gold and holding a small Nike.
Alan LeQuire recreated a sculpture by this artist in the naos of a William Crawford Smith project in Centennial Park, Nashville.
a massive chryselephantine sculpture of Athena at its center, whose ivory panels would have been brightly painted.

clues.clue: Pericles and Phidias appear on the circular shield; an Amazonomachy is also on the shield
examples: This sculptor included an Amazonomachy on the shield of his colossal Athena in the Parthenon.
Another work depicting this figure featured an image of its creator as a bald old man about to throw a large stone as Pericles fights by his side.

clues.clue: Triple-crested helmet with a sphinx flanked by two pegasi; a coiled snake between her shield and feet; wears a snake-tasseled Aegis
examples: Centaurs fought Lapiths on the sandals of this figure, whose triple-crested helmet featured a sphinx flanked by two pegasi.
A large, sacred coiled snake resided between a circular shield and this figure's feet.

examples: A frieze depicting the birth of Pandora was omitted from a 'Varvakeion' copy of a sculpture of this figure.

works.name: Elgin Marbles / Parthenon Sculptures
works.description: Phidias supervised the creation of the sculptural program of the Parthenon, including the frieze depicting the Panathenaic procession, the pediment groups (birth of Athena; contest of Athena and Poseidon), and the metopes (battle of Centaurs and Lapiths). The Elgin Marbles were removed by Lord Elgin and are now at the British Museum. Lawrence Alma-Tadema painted Phidias presenting the Panathenaic frieze to his friends. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Supervised the creation of the Elgin Marbles; Parthenon frieze depicted the Panathenaic procession
examples: The Elgin Marbles were created under the supervision of this sculptor.
A Lawrence Alma-Tadema painting depicts this artist presenting a frieze of the Panathenaic games to his friends.

clues.clue: Paul Delaroche's Hemicycle at the École des Beaux-Arts depicts Phidias (alongside Ictinus and Apelles) seated on a marble throne as one of the 75 greatest artists in history
examples: This painter showed Ictinus, Apelles, and Phidias seated on a marble throne in a panoramic work meant to depict the 75 greatest artists in history

works.name: Other Sculptures / General
works.description: Phidias also created the Athena Promachos, a giant bronze statue of Athena on the Acropolis. He created a statue of Apollo Parnopios after the god drove locusts from Attica. An inscription calling a wrestler beautiful appears on the little finger of one of his statues. Mark Barr named the golden ratio symbol phi after Phidias's name. In the contest to sculpt a wounded Amazon for the Temple of Artemis, Phidias came second to Polykleitos.

clues.clue: Name inspired Mark Barr to use phi (the Greek letter resembling Phidias's name) as the symbol for the golden ratio
examples: Although this artist likely didn't use the number, Mark Barr referenced this sculptor's name when he symbolized the golden ratio.
Mark Barr used a Greek symbol similar to this sculptor's name to represent the golden ratio.

comprehensive_summary:
Phidias (c. 480–430 BC) was the preeminent sculptor of ancient Athens, closely associated with Pericles and the Classical period. He is most commonly identified by two chryselephantine (gold and ivory) colossi: the Statue of Zeus at Olympia and the Athena Parthenos in the Parthenon.

The Statue of Zeus at Olympia was commissioned by the Eleans for the Temple of Zeus and ranked among the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World. The god was depicted seated and enthroned, holding a scepter. Pausanias reported the statue wore a glass robe and held a figurine of Nike in one hand. Ingres modeled Napoleon's pose in Napoleon I on His Imperial Throne on ancient descriptions of this work. Phidias was apocryphally murdered after completing it, and the statue required repeated application of olive oil due to the marsh conditions in the Altis Grove.

The Athena Parthenos stood about 41 feet tall in the naos of the Parthenon. Her circular shield depicted Phidias himself (as a bald old man hurling a stone) alongside Pericles, as well as a full Amazonomachy scene. A sacred coiled snake resided between the shield and her feet. Her triple-crested helmet featured a central sphinx flanked by two pegasi. The Varvakeion marble is a surviving small-scale copy. Pericles' political enemies accused Phidias of embezzling gold intended for the statue. When the British Museum 'cleaned' the Elgin Marbles, it erased evidence of the original bright coloring.

Phidias also supervised the broader sculptural program of the Parthenon—including the Panathenaic frieze, pediment groups, and metopes. Lawrence Alma-Tadema depicted him presenting the Panathenaic frieze to his friends. He created the Athena Promachos, a giant bronze Athena on the Acropolis, and a statue of Apollo Parnopios to commemorate the god's driving locusts from Attica. In the contest to sculpt a wounded Amazon for the Temple of Artemis, Phidias came second to Polykleitos. Mark Barr used the Greek letter phi — chosen to honor Phidias's name — to symbolize the golden ratio.
